title: Gemini Agent vs Hermes Agent (NEW Google Interactions API)
summary_verdict: The harness is now the product, not the model — Google's polished vertical stack (Interactions API, Antfarm sandbox, installable skills) beats open-source on safety and ergonomics, but Hermes Agent's 200K-star DIY route is the pick if you need cross-machine handoffs and GUI control today.
summary_key_takeaways: Use Google's Interactions API as the single endpoint for both Gemini model calls and agent invocations instead of wiring up separate SDKs for chat vs. tool execution.
Route any agent that writes files or executes code through the `antfarm` remote Linux sandbox rather than a local downloads folder or system path.
Install the Gemini interaction skills package on your coding agent so it learns the current SDK patterns and model versions before you migrate.

Switch to Hermes Agent for GUI control on Windows and Linux if you need the AI to click and type inside existing desktop apps, but verify UI-element targeting per app — Hermes still guesses locations.

Skip cross-device agent handoff workflows until a discovery protocol standardizes; today the nine open-source communication protocols do not agree on how agents find each other across decentralized systems.
Benchmark your choice by `200K GitHub stars` and weekly commit cadence on Hermes, not by Gemini 2.5 vs. Claude 4 leaderboard scores — the model is the engine, the harness is the product.

Treat the Logan Kilpatrick promotion of Interactions API to GA as the trigger to consolidate your tool wiring into one endpoint instead of maintaining parallel stacks for Gemini and open-source agents.

title: Hermes Bible: You Can Copy REAL Agent Workflows
summary_verdict: Hermes Bible is a high-signal, community-built workflow index worth bookmarking today, but treat its loop outputs as drafts and add your own QC agent before trusting them.
summary_key_takeaways: Submit your own production workflow to the Hermes Bible — the knowledge base grows from user contributions, so adding yours compounds the index for power users.
Configure `agents.md` and `user.md` before anything else; if you are stuck at level 0 or 1 on the 15-level roadmap, a missing context file is almost always the cause, not a bad prompt.
Run `tmux` inside a fresh project directory and pair it with `/goal` to build a loop that survives VPS disconnects — the Bible's hidden-features page skips this entirely.

Never trust a `/goal` loop output without a second agent for fact-checking and QC; treat loop output as a draft, especially the 21 copy-paste commands across research, lead gen, content, email, operations, and dev.
Route code refactoring to `Claude Code`, `Kimi Code`, or `Kilo Code` instead of `/goal` — use Hermes as the orchestrator that delegates to those harnesses, not as the coder.
Use the `Grok` + Hermes workflow for X research if you have X premium, not the X API, to avoid API billing while still pulling trending posts.
Skip loops for one-off creative work and reserve them for recurring system checks and timed research digests — the `kanban dispatcher` and `memory` loops are the highest-leverage starters.

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.17 is HUGE! (Reach Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes v0.17 is a meaningful step toward an always-on automation hub — the background sub-agents alone justify updating, but the new messaging surfaces are a second-tier feature you should only wire up for simple tasks.
summary_key_takeaways: Run `hermes update` first — every feature below requires v0.17.
Trigger async work with `delegate task background=true` whenever a task spans more than three scopes, per the creator's own threshold.
Treat image-to-image editing as iteration, not one-shot — expect 2–3 attempts unless you wire in your own `GPT image` API key.
Skip WhatsApp unless you have a `business meta account`; personal numbers are blocked.
Default to TUI for token-heavy work — messaging surfaces like iMessage and WhatsApp run a degraded version of Hermes, not full-strength.
Use the `RAFT agent network` integration to expose Hermes as a gateway node for other agents without leaking full message contents.
Try `GLM 5.2`'s 1M token context window for long-context jobs before reaching for smaller-window models.

title: Why You MUST Secure Your Hermes Agent And How to Do It
summary_key_takeaways: The simplest Hermes agent deployment option exposes the dashboard to the public internet by default, making it the riskiest setup if left unsecured.
An unprotected dashboard allows attackers on the network to sniff transmitted data and attempt brute force attacks to gain unauthorized access.
A successful breach can give attackers access to API keys, configuration files, and the ability to execute shell commands acting as the legitimate agent owner.
The recommended solution is to place the dashboard behind Tailscale, a free VPN service that restricts access to authenticated devices on a private network.
Using Tailscale is described as the 'free method' for securing the deployment, making robust security accessible without additional cost.
VPN or Tailscale protection is non-negotiable for this deployment method—without it, the agent is fundamentally vulnerable to remote takeover.

title: How to Connect Hermes Agent VPS to Desktop App (FREE Guide)
summary_verdict: Worth attempting only as a local-dev convenience on Tailscale — the connection works but the desktop app bleeds tokens, lacks a file browser, and may drop within minutes of logout, so keep your VPS agent on TUI.
summary_key_takeaways: Use Tailscale — never expose the Hermes dashboard port (default 9191) directly to the public internet, or API keys and shell access are one brute-force away.
Stay on the username/password method only if you're on the Nuki **basic** plan; if you upgrade to the **plus** tier, switch to OAuth and skip the VPN overhead.
Pin desktop app to **version 0.16** or newer — older builds show a `session token` field and will not render the gateway sign-in form.
Keep the username as `admin` and `chmod 600` the `.env` file after the heredoc closes with `EOF`, so the OIDC secret isn't world-readable.

Persist the dashboard with `tmux new -s hermes -d 'hermes dashboard --no-open --bind 100.x.x.x:9191 …'` — expect a roughly 50% chance it survives a logout, so don't rely on it for unattended 24/7 use.
Keep coding/terminal sessions on the TUI client — the desktop app dilutes tokens with UI-schema overhead, costing you budget on every prompt.
Accept the missing file browser: the desktop sidebar shows the local filesystem, not the VPS, so use Termius SFTP for remote file work.

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.16 is HUGE! (Surface Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes v0.16 'Surface' is a meaningful UX release — use the desktop app for project overviews and the TUI for token-efficient coding work, but skip the desktop for MCP-heavy sessions until the discoverability PR ships.
summary_key_takeaways: Run the desktop app only for project overviews; stay in the TUI for any task where token cost matters, since the desktop UI is not token-efficient.
Do not rely on the desktop app for MCP-heavy workflows until the open PR for desktop MCP discoverability lands.
Use the web dashboard's new system tab for messaging channel, MCP catalog, webhook, and credential changes instead of editing config files by hand.
Install Hermes with the blank-slate option if you do not want built-in skills, and keep the Skills Hub's `Nvidia skills` source enabled only if you actually use CUDA-X or NIM.
Treat the new `undo` command as your primary correction tool — it rolls back several user turns, which is faster than re-prompting.
Watch your read-file token usage; the `14%` reduction is automatic, but combine it with the new prompt-size diagnostic to catch context bloat early.

Rethink your prompting workflow — Anthropic's Boris Cherny and Peter Steinberger both argue you should `build a system that prompts itself` rather than prompt coding agents directly, which matches Hermes' uncapped spawn depth and `/goal` loop changes in v0.16.

title: Hermes Agent Desktop App vs TUI (Which One to Use?)
summary_verdict: Use the Hermes desktop app for orchestration and human-in-the-loop management, but keep TUI open for actual coding — desktop bleeds tokens on UI schemas and isn't worth the cost on token-heavy dev work.
summary_key_takeaways: Replace any Discord or Telegram Hermes setup with the desktop app immediately — it reads from the same config, profiles, and state database, so sessions are portable.
Switch to TUI before starting any token-heavy coding session to avoid the UI schema and background file-scanning token bleed the desktop app adds per request.
Use the TUI's `Ctrl-X` live session switcher and non-blocking input to fire off commands rapidly instead of waiting for the desktop app's session to catch up.
Point the desktop app at a remote VPS back end through the settings panel if your agent runs on a dev server, and run TUI in thin-client mode for the same VPS during coding.
Check Settings → About in the desktop app for one-click updates instead of reinstalling through the Python CLI.
Verify your `video_analyze` tool set is enabled in the desktop UI after Hermes updates — disabled tool sets won't reactivate on their own.
Don't pay for a model when a free one is in rotation — check the provider dropdown weekly, as DeepSeek V2 Flash was free for 2 days and Step 3.7 Flash is current.

title: Turning Hermes Agent Skills to Dashboards (Why This is BETTER)
summary_verdict: Build a dashboard for any multi-step pipeline you run daily; the 40 minutes vs 4 days gap is real, but the deterministic output is worth the auth and edge-case grind.
summary_key_takeaways: Use a dashboard for any pipeline you run daily — the deterministic TypeScript execution beats a probabilistic Hermes skill that "sometimes lies to you that it ran something."
Plan for 4 days of build time, not 1 hour, when converting a skill — authentication, role-level security, and double-click protection are the real blockers, not the AI logic.
Guard every button with a "is already running" check — a 3-day-old stale run can block the entire pipeline from starting.
Block concurrent runs explicitly to avoid RAM blowups and YouTube API quota exhaustion on the same project.
Skip Hermes for quota-bound APIs like the YouTube Data API — it logged 20 views when the real count was 4,000 in the demo.
Move from Hermes to a cron-triggered dashboard once the task is stable — no tokens are spent per run, only on the initial AI-assisted build.

Treat any routine data job as a database-first problem: once videos, transcripts, and comments live in Supabase you can chain follow-up actions like "convert this into an article" without re-running an agent.

title: Tips to get better at Using Hermes Agent (Live Tutorial)
summary_verdict: Your Hermes agent going dumb is almost always a workspace and context problem, not a model problem — wipe the VPS, reinstall the OS, set up project folders with their own `agents.md`, and feed the official docs before you do anything else.
summary_key_takeaways: Reinstall the VPS OS before you touch Hermes config when a long-running agent starts hallucinating — it takes minutes and removes months of accumulated state in the Ubuntu directory.
Always create one `workspace` directory with its own Git repo as a sibling to the runtime `.hermes` folder, never run multi-project work inside a single chat session.
Put one `agents.md` in every project folder and one in the Ubuntu directory — the top-level file is architecture, the project files are conventions, mirroring `cloud.md` in Cloud Code.
Paste the full Hermes docs and the GitHub repo into the agent's context on every fresh setup, and instruct it to source troubleshooting info `only` from official documentation to cut hallucinations.
Run the agent in verbose mode so you can verify it's actually doing `file extract` and `web search` against the docs instead of guessing — if you don't see those calls, the habit hasn't stuck.
If you're migrating from Open Claw to Hermes Agent on an existing VPS, expect `a lot slower` performance until you reinstall the OS; don't try to debug in place.

title: Hermes Agent Just Got FASTER
summary_key_takeaways: The Hermes team shaved off approximately one second from the overall launch time in just eight days since the previous speed upgrade video
The 'Hermes --version' command dropped from 700ms to 258ms, representing a reduction of over 63%
The performance metric is highlighted because it reflects improvements users can actually feel, not just synthetic benchmark gains
Speed improvements are noticeable not only at startup but also during mid-conversation interactions with the agent
Tool call execution feels faster and smoother, improving the overall agent experience
The rapid iteration cycle of 8 days demonstrates the team's commitment to continuous performance optimization
Both initial launch and ongoing operational performance have been improved, addressing the complete user experience

title: Optimizing AI Agent Performance (Hermes Agent v0.15)
summary_key_takeaways: Hermes Agent v0.15 reduced function call overhead from 399,000 to 213,000 calls per turn, a reduction of approximately 47%
The reduction in overhead allows Hermes to spend more computational resources on actual user tasks rather than internal machinery
Performance improvements are most impactful for longer conversations where cumulative overhead previously compounded
The optimization targets the 'hot path'—the critical execution code that runs on every turn
This update represents a focus on efficiency and lightweight operation as core development priorities
The per-turn framework overhead reduction is a significant architectural improvement in the v0.15 release

title: Hermes Agent V0.15: The 'Velocity' Release Update
summary_key_takeaways: Hermes Agent v0.15 is officially named the 'Velocity' release
This update covers five major areas: architecture, speed, task orchestration, integrations, and security
The release represents a bundled update approach rather than frequent incremental patches
The team rolls out feature highlights individually before bundling them into official releases
The release strategy aims to prevent users from being overwhelmed with constant daily updates
The 'Velocity' branding suggests performance and speed are primary focus areas
Existing users should review the changelog and upgrade to access all bundled improvements

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.15 is POWERFUL! (Velocity Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes Agent v0.15 is a real architectural cleanup — the creator's view is that bundling small upgrades into one release beats daily churn, and this one delivers measurable speed wins plus a session-search fix worth the upgrade on its own.
summary_key_takeaways: Upgrade now if you've been avoiding Hermes because session search was a 90-second blocker — it now runs in 20ms with no extra LLM cost.

Use `hermes kanban swarm` for any multi-step coding job that branches into setup, verification, and parallel subtasks — the work-tree-per-task setup means you can run multiple fixes in parallel without collisions.
Replace raw `env` file sprawl with the new **Bitwarden** bootstrap-token integration if you rotate provider keys frequently — one token pulls secrets cleanly.
Use `hermes sessions list` and resume a session ID to read the full verbatim conversation instead of parsing noisy logs full of tool calls and hex values.
Don't enable Notify or other messaging platforms on Hermes — the creator recommends staying on TUI/CLI and leaving messaging use cases to OpenClaw.
Pin to v0.15 + the same-day hot fix together; the hot fix restores the 19,000-entry skills catalog and the Docker MCP `bear` command, so running v0.15 alone will look broken.
Audit your agent's exposure to untrusted tool output, recalled memory, and stored skills — v0.15's brainworm mitigations add 15 new threat patterns and a security guidance plugin worth enabling.

title: Hermes Agent’s HUGE New Update: Built-in MCP Catalog!
summary_key_takeaways: Hermes Agent now features a built-in MCP catalog that enables one-click installation of pre-approved MCP servers
The feature is disabled by default and must be manually enabled through the update and catalog commands
Currently approved MCP servers include N8N, Linear, and GitHub, though only Linear and N8N are actively available
The catalog eliminates the need to manually edit configuration YAML files to wire up MCP servers
All MCP servers in the catalog are personally vetted and approved by the Hermes team for security
Users can enable the feature by running 'Hermes update' followed by 'Hermes MCP catalog'
This update significantly reduces setup friction for users new to MCPs who lack technical configuration experience
The built-in catalog provides agents with native access to integrated platforms without complex setup procedures

title: 4 Hermes Agent Prompts You MUST Know
summary_key_takeaways: The 'Hermes update' command pulls the latest catalog manifests, ensuring access to current tools and servers
'Hermes MCP' launches the available list of MCP servers for installation
'Hermes MCP install' only works for servers present on the manifests, making the update step a prerequisite
'Hermes MCP configurer' opens a tool checklist to add or remove permissions for installed servers
These four commands form a complete workflow: update, list, install, and configure
Running the update first prevents errors and missing options during installation
The configurer provides granular permission control for security and functionality
Memorizing these four prompts allows for efficient navigation of the Hermes Agent system

title: Hermes Agent MCP Catalog Guide (NEW)
summary_verdict: Hermes' built-in MCP catalog is an underrated, safer on-ramp for new users — but the current snapshot only ships Linear and n8n, so power users can skip it until GitHub lands.
summary_key_takeaways: Run `hermes update` before `hermes mcp` — catalog manifests are never auto-synced, so out-of-date snapshots will hide newly approved servers.
Stick to `hermes mcp install` for your first MCPs and only branch into hand-edited `config.yaml` files once you understand the tool-whitelist model.
Whitelist individual tools at install time and re-run `hermes mcp configure <name>` to revoke them later — never expose the full surface of an MCP server.
Treat any server outside the `optional/mcps` directory as unvetted; the catalog exists specifically because "some of them might not be safe" and users have been attacked.
For Linear, store OAuth tokens in `~/.hermes/mcp_tokens/linear.json`; for n8n, the base URL and API key go in `~/.hermes/.env` and Hermes probes the instance automatically.
Wait on the GitHub MCP until it ships — it's already listed in the manifest but disabled, and install is expected to take a personal access token scoped to your repos.
If you already run a custom MCP setup, skip the catalog — Ron calls it an update "more so for the everyday users, the casual users, the beginners."

title: DeepSeek v4 Flash + Hermes Agent: Stop wasting time configuring AI agents
summary_key_takeaways: Manual configuration of Hermes Agent is time-consuming and not ideal for everyday users who want to focus on building rather than fixing
Hostinger VPS is presented as the recommended deployment solution for users who want to minimize setup overhead
Hostinger sponsors the video and provides a ready-made Hermes Agent Docker template as the key deployment tool
The Docker template automatically handles the entire deployment infrastructure, eliminating manual setup steps
Using a VPS ensures a stable, reliable, and always-on environment for running Hermes Agent
The approach abstracts away complex tasks like dependency management and environment configuration from the end user
This deployment method prioritizes developer experience by removing friction from the setup process

title: How to summarize long documents instantly with AI (DeepSeek v4 Flash + Hermes Agent)
summary_key_takeaways: Use DeepSeek v4 Flash combined with Hermes Agent to summarize long documents like 100-page dissertations instantly
Avoid feeding URLs directly to the AI, as most agents will only scrape abstracts, summaries, or overviews rather than the full document
Save source documents as PDF, markdown, or HTML format to ensure the AI has access to complete content
Create a dedicated folder in your working directory to organize both source documents and AI-generated outputs
The demonstration used two PDFs and one HTML document focused on AI topics
Use a structured three-part prompt: read the documents, extract the top five action items from each, and write a merged summary in the same folder
This workflow is ideal for researchers, students, and professionals who need to quickly distill insights from lengthy academic or technical documents

title: How to properly test your AI agent Hermes Setup
summary_key_takeaways: Running Hermes TUI is essential for conducting proper tests of the AI agent
The reasoning parameter must be set to 'X high' (the highest available setting) to match benchmark conditions
Verbose mode should be enabled by cycling through options until 'verbose equals verbose' is displayed
Published V4 Flash benchmarks are cherry-picked from runs performed at maximum effort, so configuration must match
The first test is a stress test measuring requests per minute throughput
A Python script will be fed to Hermes to test both performance and refactoring capabilities
Combining throughput testing with functional code review tasks provides comprehensive agent evaluation
Configuration verification is a prerequisite step before any meaningful benchmark testing

title: Agent Harness vs Coding Harness (Know the Difference)
summary_verdict: Use coding harnesses (Claude Code, Kilo Code) for any code-producing work; treat general agent harnesses (Hermes, OpenClaw) as long-running assistants that improve only with hundreds of hours of your own feedback — pick by loop, not by model.
summary_key_takeaways: Use Kilo Code when starting a full-stack app from scratch — it is bring-your-own-key and beats Hermes for code with verifiable outputs in the same model comparison.

title: How to set up DeepSeek V4 Flash in Hermes Agent
summary_key_takeaways: Run the Hermes update first to ensure the platform recognizes the new V4 Flash model configuration
Type 'Hermes model' to access the model selection menu after updating
Select the 'News Portal subscription' option, which provides free input, output, and caching capabilities
The free tier displays the pricing as 'in, out, and cache is free' in the model list
For normal use, simply run 'Hermes' as the standard command
For testing rate limits and tool calls, use 'Hermes TUI' instead of the default 'Hermes' command
Hermes TUI provides real-time visibility into tool calls and rate limit behavior, which is essential for performance testing
The TUI mode is specifically recommended for the purpose of evaluating model behavior and rate limit triggers

title: DeepSeek v4 Flash + Hermes Agent = Surprisingly STRONG
summary_verdict: DeepSeek V4 Flash is a legitimately strong lightweight model worth routing most agent tasks through, but sub-agent delegation is too slow and vision/one-shot tasks are weak.
summary_key_takeaways: Route Python refactors, repo mapping, and document summarization to V4 Flash before touching V4 Pro — it matched Pro's output on 5 coding problems at 1/4 the cost.
Set Hermes TUI reasoning to `xhigh` and verbose to `verbose` to avoid being benchmarked at a lower tier than the public numbers.
Cap single-prompt file count around 60 iterations (default 60/60 budget) and under ~500 lines of Python to stay clear of the free-tier rate limit.
Skip sub-agent delegation on V4 Flash for research tasks — the creator's run took 14 minutes vs ~2 minutes when feeding the same docs directly to the main agent.
Save Google Scholar papers as PDF/HTML into a dedicated folder, never pass the URL — Hermes scrapes only the abstract otherwise.
Reserve V4 Pro / Gemini 2.6 / Opus for codebases you don't understand, critical thinking, and one-shot creative work; V4 Flash's 1M context + hybrid attention handles everything else.
Use prompt caching on repeat context — V4 Flash is the highest-consumed token on Hermes Agent this month precisely because cached reads are nearly free.

title: Hermes Agent: The 20 Turn Reality Check
summary_key_takeaways: The 20-turn limit in Hermes Agent applies to turns where the agent gets something wrong or gets stuck in a loop, not to normal operations
Turns increment as the second, third, and subsequent attempts when the agent makes mistakes or enters repetitive loops
The max turns setting can be modified in the Hermes config.yaml file under the goals block
If the config option doesn't appear after updating Hermes, users can ask the agent itself to add it per official documentation
The default max turns is set to 20, but can be increased in increments of 5 or 10 (e.g., 25 or 30)
Users should not attempt to raise the limit beyond 100 turns, as this is almost certainly not supported
Raising the turn limit is recommended when the Hermes agent frequently makes errors or struggles with tasks
The configuration is found by scrolling down the goals block in the config.yaml file after updating Hermes
Increasing turns provides the agent more opportunities to self-correct when handling complex or error-prone tasks

title: OpenHuman Honest Review: Is it Better than Hermes Agent?
summary_verdict: OpenHuman is currently broken in beta and not a Hermes Agent replacement — wait for it to ship, and only switch if you need one-click OAuth for office apps like Gmail, Slack, Notion, GitHub, Jira and Linear.
summary_key_takeaways: Don't switch from Hermes Agent today — OpenHuman is beta, hits a Google sign-in loop, and fails with `real-time socket is not connected` in the chat.
Re-evaluate OpenHuman in 4–8 weeks once the socket-handshake bug is fixed and the 118 Composio OAuth connectors (GitHub, Jira, Linear, Notion, Slack, Google Docs) are actually usable end-to-end.
Choose OpenHuman only if your daily work is Gmail + Slack + Notion + Google Docs — that is exactly the white-collar use case the project's own demo video markets.

Stick with Hermes Agent for any TradingView or trading workflow: there is no OAuth for TradingView, and the previous computer-use demo secretly called the CoinGecko API instead of reading the desktop app.
Start with the $1 free credit before committing to OpenHuman's pay-as-you-go or subscription — credits are required just to send the first message.
Use Hermes Agent's computer-use only for short, scripted UI flows — it still guesses pixel coordinates and breaks on CSS changes, so don't depend on it for long workflows.
Run Hermes Agent on a VPS or Docker, not only locally, if you want a desktop-app feel without giving up the CLI's natural-language cron, sub-agents, and Kanban orchestrators.

title: Hermes Agent NEW Skill: The Simplest Explanation of Skill Bundles
summary_key_takeaways: Skill bundles allow users to group multiple skills under a single slash command, simplifying complex workflows
Bundles are stored as YAML files in the .hermes/skill_bundles directory
The .hermes/skill_bundles directory is automatically created only when you make your first skill bundle
The workflow name in the YAML file becomes the slash command (e.g., 'back_end_dev' becomes /back_end_dev)
Users can include multiple skills in one bundle, such as GitHub code review, test-driven development, and GitHub PR workflow
The feature requires the latest Hermes update to be available
Skill bundles reduce the need to invoke individual skills one at a time
The YAML format is simple and human-readable, making bundle creation accessible to non-technical users

title: Hermes Agent NEW Skill: It Built Two Skill Bundles Without Being Asked
summary_key_takeaways: Hermes Agent autonomously created two skill bundles instead of the one requested—a general research bundle and a lightweight quick research bundle
Both bundles are stored in the .hermeskillbundle directory for easy access
The YAML configuration file includes five key components: name, skills, description, instructions, and general rules
Only 'name' and 'skills' are required fields; 'description' and 'instructions' are optional but recommended for better usability
The general rules section provides additional customization parameters for bundle behavior
A test command '/research find out why hyperliquid token is pumping' was used to verify the bundle functionality
The agent demonstrated proactive intelligence by anticipating different research use cases without being explicitly asked

title: Hermes Agent NEW Skill: The Missing Hermes Feature Everyone Wants
summary_key_takeaways: Hermes Agent has launched a new skill bundling feature that users have been requesting
Currently, skill bundles must be created via command-line rather than a drag-and-drop interface on the dashboard
A drag-and-drop dashboard interface for skill bundling is planned for a future Hermes update
Users can consult with their Hermes agent to get AI-driven recommendations for which skills to bundle together
A general research workflow requires skills across five phases: discovery, extraction, organization, analysis, and output
The agent recommended creating a single umbrella skill to consolidate all research-related capabilities
Total skills needed for research workflow: 4 discovery + 3 extraction + 3 organization + 3 analysis + 5 output = 18 skills

title: Hermes Agent NEW Skill Bundles (Setup Guide)
summary_verdict: Skill Bundles are a real workflow fix for Hermes — group skills once, invoke with one slash command instead of hoping the agent picks all five subtasks from one prompt.
summary_key_takeaways: Use `/bundles create` and name the workflow after the slash command you want — e.g. workflow name `research` becomes `/research`.
Put house rules in the YAML `instructions` field instead of repeating them in every prompt — treat it as a `mini agents.md`.
Consult your Hermes agent first and review its bundle plan before accepting it, because skill ordering is not documented as adaptive.
Commit your `~/.hermes/skill-bundles/*.yaml` files to a shared dotfiles repo so the team gets the same workflows.
Run `/bundles reload` after manual YAML edits instead of restarting Hermes.
Watch for the context-window bug when switching models — DeepSeek V4 Pro inherited the Kimiko 2.6 window in the creator's setup; hard-code the value in config until Hermes fixes it.
Ask the agent to propose a lightweight variant alongside the full bundle — the demo produced a `quick-research` from one `general-research` request.

title: Hermes Agent NEW Skill: Are Bundled Agent Skills Actually Adaptive
summary_key_takeaways: The central question explored is whether Hermes Agent's bundled skills run sequentially in a fixed order or can be adaptively rearranged based on context
Current official documentation does not explicitly state whether bundled skills are adaptive, leaving users to discover this through testing
Users are advised to consult directly with their Hermes agent to determine the optimal workflow arrangement before deploying bundles
The Hermes agent itself can analyze and arrange the skill order according to the requirements of the specific workflow
Bundle management is available through the Hermes CLI, including the ability to create new bundles
A 'list' command is available in the CLI to view how many bundles a user currently has configured
The video was recorded while waiting for a research report, indicating bundled skills were being tested in a real research workflow
Adaptive execution versus sequential execution significantly impacts how flexible and useful bundled skills are in dynamic production scenarios

title: NEW Hermes Agent Speed Upgrade (Update NOW)
summary_verdict: Update immediately — Hermes Agent's startup dropped from 10–15s to 2–3s and tool latency collapsed from a 200ms fixed tick to 5ms adaptive polling, four small PRs stacking into a real speed win.
summary_key_takeaways: Update Hermes Agent before benchmarking it — cold-start times of 10–15s are no longer representative.
Run multiple Hermes workers on a VPS only after pulling PR 28864; the 30MB-per-process SDK preload was the main bloat source.
Stress-test Hermes on a busy gateway rather than single-chat — that's where the chat-loop cleanup (400k → 213k Python calls per 31-turn run) actually pays off.
Don't compare Hermes terminal feel to Claude Code or Aquilo on a pre-update build — the old 200ms fixed tick was most of the visible tool latency.
If you script short one-off commands through Hermes, expect the biggest subjective win from PR 28957's lazy compression check (~400ms per agent construction).
Treat per-provider reasoning-pad behavior as cached, not recomputed — don't re-test it on every turn expecting fresh results.

title: Minimax Mavis: The BEST Multi-Agent Platform for Beginners
summary_verdict: Mavis Desktop is the best beginner multi-agent package available right now — cheap ($10/mo entry), no per-use fees, and pre-wired with an orchestrator, adversarial verifier, and scheduler — but it's a starting point, not a replacement for a full custom coding harness like Hermes or Claude Code.
summary_key_takeaways: Start on the **$10/mo** token plan — the creator confirmed it can run multiple full projects (trips, SEO jobs, mini-games) without topping up, unlike Claude Teams which burned 60% of a weekly quota on one trip.
Always use the **new Mavis** product, not the classic Cloud Doctor/Pocket build — the creator explicitly says the new system "seems to work a lot better."

Tell the Mavis Assistant to **"ask me any questions first before you make any changes or any actions"** on your first run — the creator flagged this as the right onboarding pattern to avoid the orchestrator going off-script.
Lean on the **adversarial verifier** for fact-checking instead of asking the same agent to fact-check its own output — it has no shared conversation history, so the review is genuinely blind.
Use the **`/` slash-command skills** menu and build your own reusable skills for repeatable workflows like SEO competitor analysis and daily news digests.
Spin up **fresh agents per sub-task** rather than extending one long thread — the creator noted "the best performance comes at the early context," so new agents beat thread continuation.
Move to **Hermes or a custom Claude Code harness** only after you outgrow Mavis — Mavis is a starter pack, not a replacement, and config customization is where the next performance gains live.

title: Hermes Just Got Real Time News Access
summary_key_takeaways: Hermes now has real-time news access through integration with Grok and X search
The update is called the 'foundation release' and is available as a free upgrade
No API key is required to use Grok through Hermes, removing a major friction point
Users can upgrade by going to the console and typing 'Hermes update'
The X search functionality allows Hermes to pull current information from the X platform
This integration eliminates the previous limitation of having outdated information from training cutoffs
The upgrade process is designed to be simple and accessible to non-technical users

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.14 is SO USEFUL (Foundation Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes v0.14 is worth the free upgrade if you already pay for X Premium Plus ($30/mo); skip the X integration otherwise and wait for the Linux/Windows computer-use rollout in the coming weeks.
summary_key_takeaways: Run `Hermes update` in the console now — v0.14 is a free upgrade and the OAuth login is a one-time step.
Subscribe to X Premium Plus ($30/mo), not base Premium, before enabling the X integration or OAuth will fail silently.
Delete the $19/mo unofficial X scraper subscription — OAuth replaces it for both search and Super Grok.
Wait for the Linux/Windows computer-use rollout "in the next few weeks" if you need desktop-app control off macOS.
Skip native Windows for now — the host still rates macOS as "probably the best way to use everything" over the new Windows build.
Run the new `debloat` command to strip unused modules like `camel flock` if your install feels bloated.
Don't pick Grok for agentic chains — the creator says it's "fast" but "not your best servant"; reserve it for X-sourced news only.

title: Grok + Hermes Agent = INSANE Combo
summary_verdict: Worth turning on immediately if you already have X Premium Plus — it's free, OAuth-based, and gives Hermes an X search channel that would otherwise cost extra API spend.
summary_key_takeaways: Pick `X Grok` from the Hermes model list and authorize via browser OAuth — do NOT paste an API key, since API mode is "slower" and pay-per-call.
Use Grok inside Hermes specifically for the X search and `imagine` image-gen features; skip it for general reasoning because quality is described as "not the strongest."
Subscribe to X Premium Plus ($30/mo equivalent in the creator's experience) over a standalone Grok subscription if you also want reply boost on X, per the creator's own reasoning.
Build bookmark-draining workflows now while OAuth limits are reportedly generous, before xAI tightens Super Grok quotas for agent traffic.
Expect Hermes — not OpenClaw — to keep receiving xAI-favored integrations for the foreseeable future; weight your agent stack accordingly.
Run a Grok 4.3 limit test on day one and report findings in the comments, as the creator explicitly asked the audience to crowdsource the real-world cap.

Hermes Agent Kanban + Cron Job is POWERFUL (Setup Guide)
summary_verdict: Hermes Kanban dramatically improves pipeline quality over single-sub-agent cron jobs, but the creator's own setup needed four custom fixes — don't pair Kanban with cron until Hermes ships a delete button and dedup logic.
summary_key_takeaways: Start your Kanban setup in a dedicated workspace folder (e.g. `AI News/`) rather than a scheduled cron — the creator would skip cron pairing entirely on a rebuild.
Paste Hermes Kanban docs links into TUI and ask *"Can you study and understand this first?"* before letting the agent configure profiles; it will skip official docs and hit Reddit otherwise.

Reuse one API key across all specialist profiles by removing empty API key fields from `config.yaml` and copying the main agent's key into each profile `.env` — useful if you're on a Kimi coding plan.
Run a systemd service to keep the Hermes gateway alive 24/7 if you're on a VPS; the creator warns local machines will rack up power costs with this fix.
Set token budgets per role: 90–100 for researchers, mid-range for editors, 20–30 for publishers — the agent will propose these values itself.

Wait for Hermes to ship a delete button and native cron-to-Kanban dedup before running scheduled jobs at scale — the creator's own setup hit 63 orphan children in one week and currently only supports `block`, not delete.
Add a pre-spawn dedup check to your cron prompt so it searches the Kanban board for same-day parent tasks before creating new ones, preventing the duplicate Discord notifications seen on May 6, 2026.

title: Hermes Agent Kanban + Cron Job: The Hidden API Key Trap in Agent Profiles
Hermes Agent Kanban UPDATE: Multiple Boards Setup
summary_verdict: Update immediately if you run the Hermes Kanban — multiple boards, profile shortcuts, and run-history retry are a real upgrade, but the isolation only works if you name your profiles per-project.
summary_key_takeaways: Update Hermes the moment you see the multi-board error — `hermes kanban boards list` returning anything other than your single old board means you're on the old Kanban.
Use the dashboard's `Copy CLI command` button on each Profile to land directly in the right `.env` in `nano` instead of guessing which key belongs to which agent.
Treat profiles as persistent roles, not sub-agents — edit `soul.md` per profile to give each one durable memory and a system prompt, which sub-agent spawns don't get.
Check the Worker Logs run history before assuming a pipeline is broken — the creator saw one task retry 81 times before succeeding, an orchestrator would have given up after run 1.
Prefix every profile name with the project slug (`ai_researcher`, `crypto_researcher`, `sports_researcher`) so workers on board ABC can't read context meant for board XYZ.
Run the daily audit script to confirm the gateway is up on VPS — a dead gateway leaves the child task waiting on the parent forever.
Pair the Kanban with the new Ink-based TUI for the closest thing to an IDE-style build loop, which the creator compares to using Opus 4.6 on Claude Code.

Hermes Agent Kanban Setup Guide (Multi-Agent Task Board)
summary_verdict: Hermes' Kanban task board is a real upgrade over blind sub-agent delegation for multi-step work, but the setup is annoyingly manual and you must create each assignee profile yourself — start with Story 1 before attempting Stories 2–4.
summary_key_takeaways: Run `hermes update` first — the Kanban release was ~8 hours old at recording, so older builds won't have the `hermes gateway start`, `hermes profile create`, or `hermes kanban` commands.

Create one Hermes profile per assignee with `hermes profile create <role>` (e.g. `researcher`, `backend-dev`); the parent agent's API key is NOT inherited, so set `inference_provider` and the key on every profile under `~/.hermes/profiles/<name>/`.
Resume a Kanban profile's TUI session with `hermes --profile <name> --resume <session_id>` — the default `hermes sessions list` only shows the main agent's sessions.

For a 5-assignee project expect 5 manual profile creations; do not expect a "spin up the Kanban and watch" hands-off flow on Story 1 — that level of automation belongs to Story 3 (multi-role pipeline with retries).

Find finished Kanban reports in `~/hermes/kanban/`, not `~/hermes/profiles/` — the worker logs and the final markdown file land in different trees and the dashboard only flips to `done` on successful write.

Treat the first run as a schema/smoke test: one assignee, one `web_search`-only task that writes a single markdown file. Only after that succeeds, bolt on paid API endpoints for extra sources beyond the free sites the default researcher hits (e.g. TechCrunch).

Stick to Story 1 (solo dev, one assignee) until it works twice in a row, then graduate to Stories 2–4 (`fleet farming`, `multi-role pipeline with retries`, `circuit breaker and crash recovery`) — each adds assignees and failure handling the boxmining demo did not exercise.

title: Here’s the Fun Part  Choosing Your API - Hermes Agent Setup on Mac
Hermes Agent Curator Guide: FIX Your Agent Skills!
summary_verdict: Curator is a genuinely useful maintenance tool for Hermes Agent skill sprawl, but expect a manual config fix and a known daemon-thread bug before it works on your setup.
summary_key_takeaways: Run `hermes update` before `hermes curator status` — the feature must be enabled in the current build, and Banner's agent exposed `43 agent-created skills` on first check.
Use `hermes curator run sync` over `hermes curator run` so you can watch the LLM pass complete — a healthy run takes 15–20 minutes, a `~1 hour` run with no report means the daemon thread died.

If `.hermes/logs/curator/` is missing, do NOT let your agent create the folder manually — feed it the official Curator docs and have it add the missing `Curator block` to the config file first, or it will hallucinate the report.
Treat the `LLM final summary` block in `report.md` as the source of truth — it lists `patches`, `absorbs`, `demotions`, and the restore command for any skill you need back.
Pre-stage `hermes curator restore <skill-name>` muscle memory: Curator never deletes, it only archives, but Banner lost active skills on the first pass and had to restore by name.

Request a `top five most recently used` field in `hermes curator status` — the current view only shows `top five least recently used` with `use never`, which makes auditing a 40+ skill catalog painful.
Pin your Hermes version in CI before enabling Curator in production — the `no module named fire` daemon import error is unfixed as of this video and silently kills the LLM review phase.

title: Hermes Agent Setup on Mac: From “45 Minutes” to “Two Minutes” in Seconds
Hermes Agent Setup on Mac (Local Install Guide)
summary_verdict: Hermes local setup on a fresh Mac works in under 5 minutes once Xcode CLT 26.4 is installed, but the creator frames the local route as a 'big pain' compared to cloud alternatives and the Kimi K2.6 path is gated by geography.
summary_key_takeaways: Install Xcode Command Line Tools 26.4 via `xcode-select --install` and the System Settings → Software Update prompt before running the Hermes one-liner, or the install stops at `command not found`.
Choose `Quick setup` in the Hermes wizard, not full setup, so you can bolt on skills and workflows incrementally instead of rebuilding from scratch.
Select the international Kimi K2.6 endpoint in the coding plan menu unless you are in China — the same geographic split applies to MiniMax models.
If the Hermes TUI loads with errors and no input box, terminate the session, open a new terminal, and run `hermes` again; a reply to `hi` confirms the install is working.
Default the chat model to `Kimi 2.6` only if your API key has access to the just-released version, otherwise pick the K2.5 fallback during setup.
Skip the Telegram/Discord integration step in the wizard if your goal is local TUI use — wiring those channels adds friction you don't need on day one.
Treat the local Hermes route as a 2–3 minute install but a multi-week build-out for skills and presentation pipelines; budget time accordingly or use a hosted agent for one-shot deliverables.

title: DeepSeek v4 Pro Review (Real World Tests)
summary_verdict: DeepSeek V4 Pro has best-in-class tool-call reasoning but the one-shot output is weaker than Kimi 2.6 and MiMo 2.5 Pro — use it via a Hermes-style agent, not your IDE, and budget $5 to start.
summary_key_takeaways: Set your Hermes agent's primary model to `DeepSeek V4 Pro` and run one-shot builds there before touching an IDE — the space-shooter V2 demo added an overheat mechanic that the Kilo Code version never produced.
Always close the terminal and reopen if `/new` or `/clear` flips you back to `DeepSeek Chat` after switching to V4 Pro in Hermes.
Cache-friendly: reusing the same base URL `api.deepseek.com` and similar prompts on V4 Pro reuses cached tokens, which is why 150 requests across V4 Pro and V4 Flash cost less than $0.30.
Skip Kilo Code's reasoning toggle for now — it hasn't been updated for V4 Pro, so `think high` and `think max` aren't exposed there yet even though the base URL still routes correctly.
Load $5 of API credit to start, not $20 — the creator calls $20 "a bit of an overkill" given sub-$0.30 burn on 150 real builds.
Write prompts as full specs, not vibes: V4 Pro's 40–45 minute extra build time only pays off when the prompt tells it what to think about between tool calls.
Treat V4 Pro's self-critique output as a feature you must read — unlike Kimi 2.6 which "just gets right to it," V4 Pro's chain-of-thought is the value, not the final code block.

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.11 is a GAME CHANGER! (Interface Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes v0.11 is the biggest release since v0.9 with 1,500 commits; the Ink-based TUI alone justifies switching from Discord for live work, while AWS Bedrock and sub-agent orchestration land it in enterprise territory.
summary_key_takeaways: Switch to `hermes --tui` for any live work — the live context window bar and sub-agent activity feed make Discord feel blind by comparison, but keep cron jobs on Discord where they still work better.
Use `/steer` (not `/btw`) when you want to redirect an in-flight tool call or force sub-agent spawning — it queues and injects after the next tool call without restarting the run.
Set a `max spawn depth` on sub-agent config immediately — the new orchestrator-to-worker pattern can recursively collapse your gateway if left uncapped.
Wire any existing shell script as a lifecycle hook via the new shell hook integration — no Python wrapper needed for cron or pre-tool veto logic.

If you're in an AWS shop, test the native Bedrock transport (Converse API) before evaluating Hermes against Anthropic-direct or LiteLLM — format conversion is now a first-class ABC layer, not a hack in the core run agent.
Lock down cron jobs with the new per-job enabled tool set to cap token spend — broad tool access on a 5-minute cron can burn a context window overnight.
Skip GPT-image-class work in Hermes for now — the Flux 2 Pro model in the news tool gateway "is still nowhere near as good as the GPT image 2 output" even on the paid plan.

title: Top AI Models for Hermes Agent (Tier List)
summary_verdict: GPT 5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro are the top orchestrators, GLM 5.1 and Kimi 2.5 dominate execution, while the entire Claude line is a question mark after Opus 4.6/4.7 nerfed into a one-shot space shooter fail.
summary_key_takeaways: Use `/model` mid-session to hot-swap orchestrator to executor — type it on Discord or Telegram during an active Hermes run since the v0.8 update 2 weeks ago.
Run Opus 4.7 in plan-then-swap mode: let it draft, then switch to Mimo V2 Pro or GLM 5.1 for execution after the April 17 nerf broke its one-shot game build.

Avoid GPT 5.4 mini as a solo driver — Hermes logs show silent fallback to full GPT 5.4 even when mini is configured, which unexpectedly spikes costs; use it only as a sub-agent executor with narrow scope.
Replace the $10/month News Research built-in tools with Gemini 3 Flash for free web search and URL context reading, since it has grounding built in.
Use DeepSeek 3.2 for Hermes cron scheduler jobs — its ability to "think inside tool calls" eliminates redundant reasoning passes in the 40+ tool environments where cron jobs fail most.
Self-host Nemotron 3 Super behind a Nemo Claw privacy wrapper if API rate limits or data residency are blocking deep automation runs beyond 128K context.

title: OpenClaw Update 4.15 is less broken?
summary_verdict: OpenClaw 4.15 is a stability-and-cleanup patch, not a feature release — upgrade only because the previous build was worse, and consider Hermes ($10/mo) if you need TTS, image gen, and a working memory layer.
summary_key_takeaways: Don't evaluate Opus 4.7 on the model card's ~10% SS benchmark gain — Boxmining's real-world tests show it performing at Opus 4.6 levels, so run your own workload before committing.
Use the new OAuth token health / status card as your first stop when an OpenClaw run fails silently — it now surfaces the breaks that used to require manual debugging.
Skip Honcho memory if you're starting fresh on 4.15 and go straight to LanceDB, since Honcho "was 50% done" and the model never called its stored notes.

Expect memory to remain the weak link: even with LanceDB and the new GitHub Copilot provider, Boxmining concludes "none of the memory systems work well," so don't architect critical state around any current agent memory layer.

If you're choosing between agents today, price the comparison at Hermes' $10/mo (TTS + image gen + search bundled) versus OpenClaw 4.15 (free features, but a "platform fixes" section in the changelog).
Stay on OpenClaw 4.15 only if you were already on it — Boxmining warns that migration was a pain and there's no compelling new feature pulling you in from a competing agent.
Watch the community signal "stability increases" as the de facto changelog entry for 4.15 and treat the release as a rollback-of-the-rollback, not a feature drop.

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.10 is POWERFUL! (Tool Gateway Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes Agent v0.10 is worth the $10/month News Portal subscription for the bundled tools alone, but keep bringing your own model keys so you don't get burned by per-token pricing on the agent itself.
summary_key_takeaways: Subscribe to the News Portal **basic plan at $10/month** for the tool gateway, not the pro plan — the creator's stated ceiling is $10.
Always bring your own model key (e.g. **GLM 5.1**, Kimmy, MiniMax) and route through a flat-rate coding plan to avoid News Portal's per-token charges.
If the agent errors out after enabling the gateway, re-select your model in the UI, or edit `~/.hermes/config.yaml` and flip the `provider` field away from `newsresearch`.
Enable only the four bundled tools you actually need — **Firecrawl web search, image gen, TTS, browser automation** — and leave the rest disabled to keep the gateway cheap.
Skip Opus 4.7 for now — the creator called it "meh meh" and is staying on GPT until a real reason to switch appears.
If the agents misbehave every 6–9 hours, give the **v0.10.0** tool reliability fixes a day or two to settle before rolling back; the 180-commit patch is recent.
Run Hermes on a VPS, not a local Mac mini, and don't grant it blanket system permissions — the creator frames this as the right risk trade-off given current agent security maturity.

title: Hermes Agent: See Every Skill Your AI Agent Has—Instantly
Hermes Agent’s Biggest Upgrade:  The Live Web Dashboard
OpenClaw Update 4.12 is IMPROVING!
summary_verdict: OpenClaw 4.12 ships real upgrades — active memory and Codex routing — enough to keep the lane warm, but the creator has migrated to Hermes for self-evolving skills and isn't switching back yet.
summary_key_takeaways: Type `/verbose` after every reply in 4.12 to inspect what the active memory sub-agent injected into your prompt before you trust its output.
Switch Codex/ChatGPT traffic to the new Codex route in 4.12 — it uses native threads and is fully separate from the standard OpenAI path, so coding-plan quotas apply cleanly.
Run `Gemma 4`, `Qwen 3.9`, or the `Qwen 3.5` Opus-distilled reasoning model via LM Studio for free local inference instead of paying per-token for small tasks.
Audit any container or shell-exec paths against the `BusyBox` and `ToyBox` removal from trusted safe-bins in 4.12 — if your pipeline still calls them as trusted, patch before deploying.
If you migrated to Hermes for self-evolving skills, stay there for now — OpenClaw 4.12's active memory + dreaming is good enough to keep the lane warm but not enough to switch back yet.
If you're on Anthropic's coding plan and Claude Code disabled off-management, route through the new Codex provider in 4.12 instead of fighting Claude's rate limits.
Use the new `openclaw policy preset` and `openclaw policy set` CLI to manage tool approval policies from the terminal instead of editing config files by hand.

title: Hermes Agent Dashboard (Setup Guide and Overview)
summary_key_takeaways: Run `hermes dashboard` and tunnel with `ssh -L 9119:localhost:9119` if you're on a VPS — never expose the unauthenticated `9119` port directly.

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.9 is MASSIVE! (Everywhere Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes v0.9 is the project's biggest release yet and worth updating for the dashboard and backups, but skip fast mode, don't expose port 9119 to the internet, and manage updates from the CLI instead of asking the agent to self-update.
summary_key_takeaways: Update via `Hermes update` on the CLI and run the doctor command afterwards instead of asking the agent to self-update — breaking changes affect how Hermes runs.
Never expose port 9119 to the public internet; the creators cite a `12 seconds` SSH-attack window as realistic.
Skip `/fast` mode unless latency matters more than budget — the hosts confirm it can cost `double the cost` for the same intelligence.
Run `Hermes backup` and `Hermes import` on a schedule to replace any manual cron job for config, session, skills, and memory snapshots.
Use the new `plugins` command to swap in custom context-engine logic instead of relying on defaults, since Hermes `makes the assumption that everything is going to` fail.
Migrate from OpenClaw to Hermes for the vocal background process monitor — it surfaces errors and port-listen events inline, which compounds the savings once the self-evolving skills loop kicks in.
Treat the 5-day, 487-commit release cadence as the new normal and check commit-behind status on every boot rather than waiting for version announcements.

title: OpenClaw to Hermes Agent: The “Holy Grail” Tool Trap
OpenClaw Update 4.9: Dreaming Actually WORKS Now!
summary_verdict: OpenClaw's 4.9 dreaming update is the biggest change of the month and finally delivers on auto-promoted memory, but expect a raw, debug-heavy feature that they have 'gutted, remade like eight times' — and the creator's team is moving new work to Hermes while keeping legacy agents on OpenClaw.
summary_key_takeaways: Enable the `dreaming is on` toggle on your control panel now and budget credits for the dream cycle, but treat the feature as raw — manually inspect dream files until automation lands.
Map your own high-priority instructions to the `promoted` signal type so they outrank your historical behavior (e.g., diet switches, recurring deadlines) before the next agent run.

Use the four dream types — `short-term`, `long-term`, `grounded signals`, `promoted` — to tag instructions by decay rate, and lean on `deep sleep` to boost weighted rules like dietary restrictions above raw order history.
Pair this release with the `LLM wiki` feature so promoted memories also feed a persistent profile of your preferences, mirroring the Honcho integration Hermes charges for.

Do not migrate stable production agents off OpenClaw 4.9 yet — the creator's own team is keeping legacy skills on it to avoid a ~6-hour migration cost per agent, and is routing only new builds to Hermes.
Audit the debug dream files in 4.9 at least weekly; the team has rewritten the dreaming pipeline roughly 8 times in the past week and breaking changes are likely.

If you need self-evolving skills and verbose reasoning logs today, pilot Hermes + Honcho in parallel; if you need free promoted-memory on existing agents, stay on OpenClaw 4.9 and wait for the next dreaming rewrite.

title: Hermes Agent Update v0.8 is HUGE! (Intelligence Release)
summary_verdict: Hermes Agent v0.8 is a meaningful production-readiness upgrade — live model failover, async job notifications, and remote execute_code are worth the upgrade, but the Discord approval-button and Xiaomi Mimo v2 Pro additions are minor.
summary_key_takeaways: Use the `notify on complete` flag on any Hermes task you currently poll — it cuts idle agent time and lets you batch other work while jobs run.
Configure a fallback model now even if you only run one provider — the OpenRouter/Nous Portal auto-failover is the clearest reason to upgrade from OpenClaw.
Switch the browser tool from `browserbase` to `browseruse` and add Firecrawl as a cloud option before any scraping job; both ship in v0.8.
Enable shared thread sessions on Telegram/Discord gateways to skip the per-user `/start` setup that the creator called `super annoying` pre-v0.8.
Run MCP workloads only after confirming OAuth 2.1 PKCE is enforced and the OSV vulnerability scan is enabled — both land in this release.
Skip the new `/approve` clickable button on Discord if you scripted around the old typed command; it's a UI change with no API difference.
Audit your Hermes docs against v0.8 — 40+ discrepancies were just fixed, so any tutorial older than this release is suspect.

title: Hermes Agent Setup on VPS
summary_verdict: A clean four-step VPS install path for Hermes, but skip the OpenClaw import and don't expect cron jobs to migrate cleanly — set up fresh.
summary_key_takeaways: Buy the 4 GB / $3 per month tier on Zebura and filter standard plans by price before buying anything bigger.
Use Termius over plain terminal so the bundled SFTP pane lets you see and edit every file on the VPS.
Run `sudo apt update` on a fresh VPS before pasting the Hermes installer, since the script depends on `git` and `curl` being present.
Answer `no` to the `OpenClaw installation detected. Would you like to import from OpenClaw?` prompt on a clean VPS — the import path silently dropped his cron jobs.
Choose `Quick setup` instead of `Full` on first install, then customize later once the agent is live.
For non-China deployments, pick the `global direct API, MiniMax` entry and set the model to `MiniMax M2.7` before any provider-specific tuning.
If you already have cron jobs on OpenClaw, do not trust the in-installer migration — wait for his separate video on the manual method he says is 99% fixed.

title: Hermes Agent UPDATE is Very POWERFUL!
summary_verdict: Hermes Agent 0.070 is the better long-term bet than Open Claw because of self-improving skills and a real credential system, but the migration is still painful and neither agent is fully secure yet.
summary_key_takeaways: Migrate to Hermes 0.070 if you run scheduled cron jobs, because Open Claw leaves them behind and the host lost their entire runtime by deleting it 'hastily'.
Move every API key and password into the new Hermes credential pool before the next push, since Open Claw stored them inside skill files that were trivially extractable from GitHub.
Use Camel Fox for flight price tracking and Amazon/competitor scraping, but skip it for X/Twitter because the platform's anti-bot detection is described as 'way out there'.
Flush the agent's memory state when you finish a project, because both Hermes and Open Claw resurface 'old memories' from weeks ago if you don't reset them.
Skip the $20 Cloud Code plan for non-trivial multi-step work — one trip-planning session consumed 30–40% of the credits — and only consider it on the $200 Max tier.
Test Xiaomi's MiMo and MiniMax models through the Hermes portal instead of defaulting to Anthropic, since the Chinese models are 'benching very very strong' at a 'drastic cost reduction'.
Treat both agents as insecure by default and re-audit after every gateway update, because 'everything needs security hardening' regardless of which platform you pick.

title: OpenClaw Update 4.7 is Actually INSANE!
summary_verdict: OpenClaw 4.7 is finally a real competitor to Claude Code and Hermes, with the new memory wiki + dreaming as the standout upgrade — but the channel is mid-migration to Hermes and still won't call OpenClaw a finished product.
summary_key_takeaways: Enable the memory wiki + Dream feature together — they ship as a pair and Dream writes into the wiki automatically, killing the manual `update your memory` prompts.
Switch image, video, music, and sound-effect generation to native calls (Runway, Grok AI, MiniMax Music) instead of writing custom skills like `nano banana`.
Replace ad-hoc memory file lookups with `openclaw infer` as the single integration point for models, web, and embeddings.
If you also pay for Claude Code, wire OpenClaw in via the new webhooks and offload minor/cheap tasks to it — keep the expensive Claude runs for the heavy lifts.
If you run an agent on OpenClaw, snapshot your session state with the new compact points before any upgrade — 4.7 is still patching the 4.1 hardcoded exec-tool regressions.

If terminal transparency and cron job visibility matter more than media features, test a Hermes migration now — the channel reports `Stark` handled the cutover, but notes it is "not a one-click" move because not everything is native.
Bring your own model — Qwen, Z.AI, Xiaomi, MiniMax are the cheap tier the channel is betting on; treat Claude Code as the premium path, not the default.

title: OpenClaw Update 3.31: But is it Enough?
summary_verdict: OpenClaw 3.31 is a solid stability and security patch but not a reason to switch from Hermes or Claude Code computer use — stick with OpenClaw only if your multi-channel ClawHub setup is already working.
summary_key_takeaways: Audit any installed ClawHub plugin against the 3.31 critical-code findings list before updating, since ~10% of ecosystem plugins have historically been flagged as malicious.
Rotate or set an explicit gateway token before deploying 3.31, because the old same-host implicit trust path is now closed.
Replace any name-based ACP tool approval overrides with semantic approval classes, and explicitly grant node-command access after device pairing.
Switch heavy cron or multi-agent setups to the new `openclaw flows list`, `flows show`, and `flows cancel` commands instead of relying on background-task UIs.
Re-enable `/by-the-way` if you disabled it — the Claude adaptive-thinking collision is fixed in 3.31 by routing side queries around provider reasoning.
Set per-server MCP connection timeouts immediately, and namespace your `server/tool` references to avoid silent collisions between servers exposing the same tool name.

Skip migrating from OpenClaw to Hermes if your cron jobs are not reproducible from scratch — the team saw unsynced cron jobs after a Loki migration, even though greenfield Hermes onboarding is reportedly under 15 minutes.

title: Computer use INSIDE Claude Code (This changes EVERYTHING)
summary_verdict: Computer use inside Claude Code is a real step toward an all-in-one MCP workstation, but it works well only on Mac, is brand new, and the Max plan still has rate-limit pain — so run it alongside Hermes, not as a full replacement yet.
summary_key_takeaways: Run the new computer-use MCP on a Mac first; on Windows, expect "doesn't work super well" and wait for a stable build.
Pair the **$10/month Hermes + Chinese model** stack for repetitive agentic tasks with **Claude Code Max** for heavy lifting, since the latest Hermes MCP build is callable from inside Claude Code.
Use computer use specifically to automate apps that ship no MCP — Descript and Photoshop are the named examples in the video.
Plan a staged migration: the creator estimates roughly **80%** of current Open Code / Hermes workflows can move to Claude Code with computer use, leaving the rest on Hermes.
Cap your Max-plan usage until Anthropic finishes the Reddit-flagged rate-limit investigation, and don't assume the stated limits are final.
Track the cadence — Boris Cherny "hasn't actually coded something since November," which signals Claude Code may be dogfooding an unreleased internal model, so expect weekly capability jumps.
Skip the Windows install for now and route the workflow through a Mac host, matching the creator's own setup (Macs for work, Windows for streaming).

title: Hermes Agent UPDATE is INSANE! (MCP Server Mode)
summary_verdict: Hermes Agent v0.060 is a real step up — MCP server mode and profile isolation are the two features worth your time, but manual migration is painful enough that you should let Claude Code handle it.
summary_key_takeaways: Connect Claude Code to Hermes v0.060 over MCP instead of scripting Hermes directly — it's the fastest way to see and debug what the agent is doing.
Use profile isolation to share one orchestrator and one presentation agent across a team instead of spinning up per-user server instances.
Enable `Yolo mode` only on throwaway profiles; it disables the hardened security prompts and Hermes agents can touch `a lot of files`.
Run the official `Hermes MCP official Docker image` if you want a `one-click` setup rather than hand-rolling per-server installs.

Do not attempt a manual migration from an older Hermes version — point Claude Code at the v0.060 docs, install dependencies first, then run the dry run or expect profiles to vanish (`only three of my profiles survived`).
Treat the v0.060 dry run as destructive: back up every profile and dependency list before kicking it off, since the Boxmining team lost most of theirs in one pass.
Skip the v0.060 release if you don't need MCP exposure or team-shared agents — the rest is `bug fixes` and locale work.

title: Anthropic's next AI model Mythos can hack you...
summary_verdict: Anthropic's pre-release of Mythos confirms a cyber-capable model tier above Opus, but their quiet rate-limit cuts and lack of usage transparency make Claude a poor primary coding stack; switch to Hermes + GLM 5.1 if you want visibility and don't need max intelligence.
summary_key_takeaways: Treat any Mythos-touchable system as pre-compromised: apply the cyber-security patches from the leaked pre-release before the public launch, not after.
Stop trusting Claude's usage screen — since it shows no requests-per-5h figure, run a controlled prompt-count baseline this week so you can detect the next silent rate-limit cut.
Subscribe to Z.AI's $10/mo GLM 5.1 coding plan as a Claude Code hedge, but run tasks during off-peak windows to avoid the post-release congestion that is still blocking WildClawBench tests.
Migrate your OpenClaw/Stark agent memory to Hermes (17k stars) to gain the every-15-prompt self-improvement loop and explicit context/memory-deletion logs that OpenClaw lacks.
Re-benchmark GLM 5.1 yourself against the 5.0 baseline of 33% on WildClawBench before accepting the Z.AI "close to Opus" claim — the channel's 36% number is partial.
Skip the Claude Code Max $200/mo tier unless you need state-of-the-art intelligence, because the plan comes with no usage transparency and confirmed rate-limit reductions.
Hold DeepSeek evaluations until they ship a model — three months of papers only means their next release is the one to test, not the current silence.

title: Hermes vs OpenClaw: Why Everyone Is Migrating
summary_verdict: Hermes Agent is a legitimate OpenClaw replacement worth migrating to for BYOK users, but Claude Code still wins if you can stomach the $200/mo Max plan.
summary_key_takeaways: Run the built-in 'Migrating from OpenClaw' script from the Hermes GitHub repo to transfer your soul, memories, and settings instead of rebuilding from scratch.
Bring a MiniMax, Z.AI, or Xiaomi Mimo key to Hermes to keep the stack free — do not assume you need Nous Research's paid models.
If you were hand-editing JSON files to enable prompt caching on OpenClaw, stop and switch — Hermes ships prompt caching enabled by default.
Use the 15-turn evolution loop as your skill-improvement mechanism instead of manually opening scripts to refactor thumbnail or image-gen skills like the creator did the week prior.
Watch for the skill-overlap bug that hit the creator's thumbnail vs. image generator on OpenClaw — Hermes separates skills more cleanly, so audit any merged-skill agents after migration.
Skip Hermes only if you're paying $200/mo for Claude Max and don't mind Anthropic quietly lowering limits without disclosing credit usage — otherwise Hermes wins on BYOK visibility.
Wait for the creator's follow-up guides before migrating production agents — this video is preliminary testing on the same 'Stark' agent, not a hardened review.
